

# B01: Detailed Recap

created: 2026-01-20
last_edited: 2026-02-15

---

## Meeting Overview

V (Vrijen Attawar) had an introductory call with Alex Forman, co-founder of Parse — a tool that turns any website into an API. The ~15-minute conversation covered Parse's product and pricing model, V's power-user experience with Zo Computer, a strong mutual ICP alignment between Parse and the Zo ecosystem, and a commitment from V to intro Alex to Ben and Rob (Zo founders). The tone was casual, founder-to-founder, with genuine mutual enthusiasm.

## Chronological Discussion

### Opening & Travel Catch-Up (0:00–1:20)

Light rapport-building. Alex mentioned he'd been traveling for work — sponsoring the Carnegie Mellon hackathon (partly to help an engineer build points toward an O-1 visa) and visiting New York to recruit a friend to join Parse. V noted it was a heavy-meetings day. Alex reflected on how hackathons have changed since vibe coding tools emerged — four years ago you needed a dedicated front-end person, now that constraint is gone.

### Parse Product Discussion & ICP Alignment (1:20–3:48)

V explained why Parse caught his attention: as a non-technical person who recently started working with APIs through Zo, the idea of turning any web page into structured data is immediately compelling. V hypothesized that a big part of Parse's ICP is people who wouldn't normally set up APIs or scraping infrastructure themselves.

Alex confirmed this enthusiastically, noting it was an ICP segment he hadn't originally anticipated. He shared a vivid example: someone in the **garbage collection industry** who'd had an app idea for 20 years but could never build it — and even now, there's no developer ecosystem or APIs for that domain. Parse sees this pattern constantly — people building in industries with zero prior technical infrastructure, where no APIs exist.

### Careerspan & Unsexy Industries (3:48–4:28)

V connected Alex's observation to his own thesis at Careerspan: unsexy industries lack developer representation, which constrains problem-solving. Drew an analogy to countries that are large but don't produce elite athletes due to infrastructure gaps — similarly, the garbage collection world isn't producing elite engineers, so certain societal problems remain unsolved. The conversation surfaced a shared belief in **inclusiveness as a prerequisite for representing unsolved problems**.

### Zo Computer Deep-Dive & Integration Pitch (4:28–6:20)

V explained his relationship with Zo: started as a power user, became deeply embedded, now acts as an unpaid advocate and partnership scout. He gave a concrete demo of Zo's capabilities — syncing Fitbit data, overlaying genetic profile commentary, uploading datasets, and setting up persistent services that pull from external sources.

V made the explicit pitch: **Parse would be a perfect integration for Zo**. The Zo community is full of people becoming technical through the platform, which maps directly to Parse's non-technical ICP. V also shared his N5OS repo (pasted in chat) — a collection of ergonomic improvements to the baseline Zo system including a meeting processing pipeline — and suggested Parse could be bundled as a default integration alongside tools like Plaid.

### V's Role & Relationship with Zo (6:39–8:28)

Alex asked about V's formal relationship with Zo. V explained he's in the process of selling Careerspan and figuring out his next chapter. He scouts partnerships for Zo and intros them to Ben, but doesn't take compensation — partly for IP cleanliness since he's building on another company's platform. Everything he builds on Zo is given away for free or used privately, which aligns his incentives toward spreading knowledge and good practices rather than commercial gain.

### Parse Pricing Model & Variable Cost Challenge (9:13–11:15)

Alex revealed Parse is about to switch from monthly credit buckets to **usage-based pricing** (potentially this week). He explained the core pricing challenge: costs are wildly variable per API call. A Walmart product search for a niche item might return one page (fractions of a cent), while "headphones" returns 10,000 products with 100x the bandwidth cost — on the same API. Anti-bot costs (captchas) add another unpredictable layer. Monthly buckets were a stopgap; usage-based better matches actual value delivery.

V was visibly excited about the usage-based shift, noting it aligns perfectly with Zo's model.

### Zo + Parse Integration Vision (11:18–12:32)

V articulated a concrete integration scenario: Zo's AI "brain" could add logic around Parse's API — e.g., automatically adding products to a watch list, dropping items after two weeks of inactivity, turning one-off scraping events into persistent monitoring systems. This transforms Parse from a point tool into a **pseudo-UI with reasoning capabilities**. Alex agreed, noting LLMs eliminate the need for hardcoded rule sets.

### Next Steps & Closing (12:32–15:43)

**Committed actions:**
- V will intro Alex to **Ben and Rob** (Zo founders) via email
- V will send Alex demos of himself using Parse in the next 1-2 weeks, plus product feedback
- Alex invited V to try Parse's biggest update (released that week), acknowledged some kinks being worked out
- V suggested Alex join the **Zo Discord** (~1,000 members, heavily in Parse's ICP), with the professional caveat not to spam

Personal notes: Alex is Bay Area-based, had just made his first trip to New York. V is in Brooklyn. V offered to connect Alex with people in his network and grab a drink next time Alex is in town. Alex mentioned trying to recruit a friend in NYC who has a good living situation (Lower East Side) and is reluctant to move to SF.

## Key Takeaways

- **Strong ICP overlap**: Parse's non-technical users building in underserved industries maps directly to Zo's community of people becoming technical through the platform
- **Usage-based pricing alignment**: Parse is switching to usage-based pricing imminently, which V flagged as optimal for Zo's model
- **Integration opportunity**: V envisions Parse as a bundled integration in Zo — turning any website into an API, accessible to non-technical users with Zo providing the orchestration logic
- **Intro to Zo founders committed**: V will connect Alex with Ben and Rob for a partnership conversation
- **V as bridge**: V is positioning himself as the non-technical power user who can credibly advocate for developer tools to a broader audience — Parse fits this narrative
- **Parse's core challenge**: Variable per-call costs (bandwidth + anti-bot) make pricing extremely difficult; the move to usage-based is meant to solve this
- **Community channel**: Zo Discord (~1,000 members) identified as a natural distribution point for Parse awareness
